# Issuer field inference logic
_Exported on 2/13/2026 at 00:22:24 CST from Cursor (2.3.34)_

---

**User**

What we want to do is first extract rules from icdr document and then use the rules generated to build the issuer fields and/or its schema like issuer field and its data type and then the extraction can extract based on this schema. The problem is if the rules are not correctly extracted, there can be downstream pain but for now the rules are 90% correct, so I want to make sure the issuer fields infering from the rules json file is tight

Check the below answer by GPT 5.2 online and make changes accordingly. 

1) Major bug: SPLIT_FIELDS_RE will split identifiers incorrectly

You currently have:

SPLIT_FIELDS_RE = re.compile(r"[,\sand/]+")


Because this is a character class ([...]), it does not mean “comma OR whitespace OR the token 'and' OR '/'”. It means “split on any run of the characters: comma, backslash-s, a, n, d, slash, space …”.

Result: it can split inside valid identifiers containing letters like a, n, d, s (which is… almost all identifiers). That will silently shred field names and drop them due to your identifier check.

✅ Fix: use a non-character-class regex that matches separators as tokens:

SPLIT_FIELDS_RE = re.compile(r"\s*(?:,|\band\b|\bor\b|/)\s*", re.IGNORECASE)


If you also want to handle “and/or” explicitly:

SPLIT_FIELDS_RE = re.compile(r"\s*(?:,|\band/or\b|\band\b|\bor\b|/)\s*", re.IGNORECASE)


This one change will make your inferred field list dramatically tighter. 

infer_issuer_fields

2) Major bug: comparator stripping can delete other fields in the same “Map to …” clause

You do:

fields_str = re.sub(r"(>=|>|<|<=|\=).*$", "", fields_str).strip()


If notes look like:

“Map to holding_period >= 1 year and promoter_lock_in.”

This will truncate fields_str to holding_period and you’ll lose promoter_lock_in.

✅ Fix: split first, then strip comparators per-part:

parts = SPLIT_FIELDS_RE.split(fields_str)
for p in parts:
    p = re.sub(r"\s*(>=|<=|=|>|<).*?$", "", p).strip()
    ...


(Also reorder the alternation to put <= before < and >= before > if you keep a combined regex; otherwise it’s not catastrophic here, but it’s cleaner.)

This change preserves all fields even when one of them has a condition attached. 

infer_issuer_fields

3) Type inference: right now it will over-default to Nat and almost never produce String

You’ve declared a type vocabulary {Bool, Nat, List Nat, String}, but:

You never infer String except by manual adjustment later (comment says that).

Many issuer attributes are naturally “categorical/enum-like” or identifiers (company_name, industry, issue_type, etc.), and will be mis-typed as Nat if they appear in notes.

If your goal is “tight issuer fields from rules,” you’ll want the inference to be conservative in two ways:

only create fields when the “Map to …” evidence is strong,

avoid assigning numeric types to obviously non-numeric fields.

✅ Practical upgrade: add a string heuristic before falling back to Nat, e.g.:

name contains: name, type, category, class, sector, industry, country, currency, exchange, isin, cin, pan → String

text contains “shall be disclosed”, “name of”, “identify”, “address” → String

Right now, your fallback to Nat is going to create Lean/Pydantic mismatches later when extraction returns strings but schema says Nat. 

infer_issuer_fields

4) “Plural + 3 years ⇒ List Nat” heuristic is too brittle

You require:

if lname.endswith("s") and has_years:
    lean_type = "List Nat"


This will miss:

series fields that are not plural (e.g., revenue but still “preceding three years”),

series fields named like net_worth (singular) but still three-year.

✅ Fix: if has_years is true, make List Nat the default unless the field name strongly indicates a scalar.

A safer pattern:

if has_years: List Nat

else if “ratio/percent/count”: Nat

else booleans by prefix

else decide between Nat vs String

5) Boolean prefix list is risky (no_, sr_, qib_)
bool_prefixes = ("is_", "has_", "uses_", "no_", "sr_", "qib_")


is_/has_/uses_ are fine.

But no_, sr_, qib_ aren’t reliably booleans—they could be:

categories,

labels,

special regimes,

or something that wants an enum/string.

If you keep them, you’ll get false booleans that later force the extractor to hallucinate “true/false”.

✅ Suggestion: keep only is_/has_/uses_ as automatic Bool, and treat others with an evidence-based rule:

if the rule text is explicitly “Yes/No” / “whether” / “shall not” → Bool

otherwise leave as Nat/String depending on other cues.

6) “years” detection mixes name/text inconsistently

This clause:

if ("period" in lname or "months" in lname or "years" in texts_concat):
    lean_type = "Nat"


"years" in texts_concat will fire very often (lots of regulatory text references “years”).

That means you’ll classify many unrelated fields as “duration” Nat.

✅ Fix: require “years” to be tied to the field mention or use name-based triggers:

name contains period, tenure, duration, months, years, age → duration Nat

don’t use global "years" in texts_concat unless you also detect it near the mapped field phrase (harder, but better).

Bottom line

Not all good yet. The script will currently:

mis-extract field names due to the split regex (biggest issue),

drop fields when one field includes a comparator and another is joined by “and”,

overproduce Nat, causing schema/type friction and extractor mistakes.

If you implement only two changes, do:

fix SPLIT_FIELDS_RE to token-based splitting,

split first, then comparator-strip per token.

---

**Cursor**

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
infer_issuer_fields.py

Scan rules JSONL files, extract candidate issuer field names from `notes`
(e.g. 'Map to holding_period >= 1 year.'), and infer a simple type
for each field from a small vocabulary:

  Bool, Nat, List Nat, String

Outputs a canonical issuer fields file that can be used to generate:

  - Lean `structure Issuer`
  - Python dataclass / Pydantic model
  - Extraction targets for the RHP parser

Usage (example):

  python scripts/infer_issuer_fields.py \
      --rules data/processed/rules_os_qwen_32b_v4_post_new.jsonl \
      --out data/processed/issuer_fields.json

"""

import argparse
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from typing import Dict, List, Iterable, Tuple, Optional


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Data structures
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

@dataclass
class FieldContext:
    """Aggregated info for one candidate issuer field."""
    name: str
    rule_ids: List[str]
    texts: List[str]      # small snippets from rules["text"]
    notes: List[str]      # full notes where it appeared

@dataclass
class IssuerField:
    """Final schema entry for an issuer field."""
    name: str
    lean_type: str
    python_type: str
    description: str
    from_rules: List[str]


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Helpers to read rules JSONL
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def read_jsonl(path: str) -> Iterable[dict]:
    with open(path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        for line in f:
            line = line.strip()
            if not line:
                continue
            yield json.loads(line)


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Extract candidate field names from 'notes'
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

MAP_TO_RE = re.compile(
    r"""
    Map\sto          # literal 'Map to'
    \s+
    (?P<fields>.+?)  # everything until punctuation / end
    (?=$|[.;])
    """,
    re.IGNORECASE | re.VERBOSE
)

# Patterns inside the "fields" capture, e.g.
#  "holding_period"
#  "holding_period and capital_expenditure_utilization"
#  "promoter_lock_in, capital_expenditure_utilization"
# Use token-based separators so identifiers containing letters like
# "and" are not split apart.
SPLIT_FIELDS_RE = re.compile(
    r"\s*(?:,|\band/or\b|\band\b|\bor\b|/)\s*",
    re.IGNORECASE,
)


def extract_field_names_from_notes(notes: str) -> List[str]:
    """
    From a 'notes' string like:
      "Map to holding_period >= 1 year."
      "Map to promoter_lock_in and capital_expenditure_utilization."
    return ["holding_period", "promoter_lock_in", "capital_expenditure_utilization"]
    """
    result: List[str] = []
    for m in MAP_TO_RE.finditer(notes):
        fields_str = m.group("fields")
        parts = SPLIT_FIELDS_RE.split(fields_str)
        for p in parts:
            # Strip trailing comparators on each part individually so we
            # do not drop siblings joined by "and", "," etc.
            candidate = re.sub(r"\s*(>=|<=|=|>|<).*?$", "", p).strip()
            if not candidate:
                continue
            # keep only identifiers-like tokens
            if not re.match(r"^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*$", candidate):
                continue
            result.append(candidate)

    return result


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Aggregate field contexts
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def collect_field_contexts(rule_files: List[str]) -> Dict[str, FieldContext]:
    """
    Go through all rules in the given files, collect candidate field names
    from 'notes', and aggregate context.
    """
    fields: Dict[str, FieldContext] = {}

    for path in rule_files:
        for obj in read_jsonl(path):
            rule_id = obj.get("rule_id") or obj.get("rule_id_raw") or "UNKNOWN"
            notes = obj.get("notes") or ""
            text = obj.get("text") or ""

            field_names = extract_field_names_from_notes(notes)
            for fname in field_names:
                if fname not in fields:
                    fields[fname] = FieldContext(
                        name=fname,
                        rule_ids=[],
                        texts=[],
                        notes=[]
                    )
                ctx = fields[fname]
                if rule_id not in ctx.rule_ids:
                    ctx.rule_ids.append(rule_id)
                if text and text not in ctx.texts:
                    # keep only a few samples to avoid huge blobs
                    if len(ctx.texts) < 3:
                        ctx.texts.append(text)
                if notes and notes not in ctx.notes:
                    if len(ctx.notes) < 5:
                        ctx.notes.append(notes)

    return fields


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Heuristic type inference
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def guess_python_type(lean_type: str) -> str:
    if lean_type == "Bool":
        return "bool"
    if lean_type == "Nat":
        return "int"
    if lean_type == "List Nat":
        return "List[int]"
    if lean_type == "String":
        return "str"
    # fallback
    return "Any"


def infer_type_for_field(ctx: FieldContext) -> Tuple[str, str, str]:
    """
    Return (lean_type, python_type, description) for a field based on its name
    and a little bit of context.
    """
    name = ctx.name
    lname = name.lower()
    texts_concat = " ".join(ctx.texts).lower()
    notes_concat = " ".join(ctx.notes).lower()
    combined_text = f"{texts_concat} {notes_concat}"

    # --- Heuristic 1: Booleans by naming convention ---
    bool_prefixes = ("is_", "has_", "uses_")
    bool_text_cues = (
        "yes/no",
        "yes or no",
        "whether ",
        "shall not",
        "must not",
        "prohibited",
        "not permitted",
    )
    if lname.startswith(bool_prefixes) or any(cue in combined_text for cue in bool_text_cues):
        lean_type = "Bool"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Boolean flag: {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Heuristic 2: Ratios / percentages / counts -> Nat ---
    if ("ratio" in lname
        or "percent" in lname
        or lname.endswith("_pct")
        or lname.endswith("_count")
        or "per cent" in texts_concat
        or "%" in texts_concat):
        lean_type = "Nat"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Non-negative integer (e.g. percentage or ratio) for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Heuristic 3: multi-year numeric series -> List Nat ---
    has_years = ("preceding three years" in combined_text
                 or "preceding three full years" in combined_text
                 or "last three years" in combined_text
                 or "each of the preceding three" in combined_text)
    if has_years:
        lean_type = "List Nat"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Series of non-negative integers over multiple years for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Heuristic 4: contains 'period', 'months', 'years' -> Nat ---
    if (
        "period" in lname
        or "tenure" in lname
        or "duration" in lname
        or "months" in lname
        or "years" in lname
        or "age" in lname
        or "term" in lname
    ):
        lean_type = "Nat"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Non-negative integer duration (months/years) for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Heuristic 5: obvious strings / identifiers -> String ---
    string_name_keywords = (
        "name",
        "type",
        "category",
        "class",
        "sector",
        "industry",
        "country",
        "currency",
        "exchange",
        "isin",
        "cin",
        "pan",
        "tin",
        "address",
        "code",
        "rating",
        "identifier",
    )
    string_text_cues = (
        "name of",
        "shall be disclosed",
        "identify",
        "address",
    )
    if any(k in lname for k in string_name_keywords) or any(cue in combined_text for cue in string_text_cues):
        lean_type = "String"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Text/identifier field for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Fallback: treat as Nat (numeric) ---
    # You can later manually adjust a few to String/Enum if needed.
    lean_type = "Nat"
    py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
    descr = f"Non-negative integer field for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
    return lean_type, py_type, descr


def build_issuer_fields(
    field_contexts: Dict[str, FieldContext]
) -> List[IssuerField]:
    fields: List[IssuerField] = []
    for name, ctx in sorted(field_contexts.items()):
        lean_type, py_type, descr = infer_type_for_field(ctx)
        fields.append(
            IssuerField(
                name=name,
                lean_type=lean_type,
                python_type=py_type,
                description=descr,
                from_rules=sorted(ctx.rule_ids),
            )
        )
    return fields


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Pretty-print helpers (optional)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def emit_lean_structure(fields: List[IssuerField]) -> str:
    """
    Emit a Lean `structure Issuer` snippet as a convenience.
    You don't have to use this automatically; you can paste & edit.
    """
    lines = ["structure Issuer where"]
    for f in fields:
        lines.append(f"  {f.name} : {f.lean_type}")
    return "\n".join(lines)


def emit_python_dataclass(fields: List[IssuerField]) -> str:
    """
    Emit a Python @dataclass snippet as a convenience.
    """
    lines = [
        "from dataclasses import dataclass",
        "from typing import List, Optional",
        "",
        "@dataclass",
        "class IssuerModel:",
    ]
    for f in fields:
        # Map List[int] to proper typing
        py_type = f.python_type
        if py_type.startswith("List["):
            lines.append(f"  {f.name}: {py_type}  # {f.description}")
        else:
            lines.append(f"  {f.name}: {py_type}  # {f.description}")
    return "\n".join(lines)


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Main
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def main() -> None:
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    ap.add_argument(
        "--rules",
        nargs="+",
        required=True,
        help="One or more rules_*.jsonl files to scan for 'Map to ...' notes.",
    )
    ap.add_argument(
        "--out",
        required=True,
        help="Output path for issuer_fields.json (array of objects).",
    )
    ap.add_argument(
        "--emit-lean",
        default=None,
        help="Optional path to write a Lean `structure Issuer` snippet.",
    )
    ap.add_argument(
        "--emit-py",
        default=None,
        help="Optional path to write a Python dataclass snippet.",
    )
    args = ap.parse_args()

    # 1. collect field contexts
    ctxs = collect_field_contexts(args.rules)
    if not ctxs:
        print("No fields found via 'Map to ...' in the provided rules files.")
        return

    # 2. infer types
    issuer_fields = build_issuer_fields(ctxs)

    # 3. write issuer_fields.json
    out_data = [asdict(f) for f in issuer_fields]
    with open(args.out, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        json.dump(out_data, f, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2)
    print(f"Wrote {len(issuer_fields)} issuer fields to {args.out}")

    # 4. optionally emit helper snippets
    if args.emit_lean:
        lean_src = emit_lean_structure(issuer_fields)
        with open(args.emit_lean, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
            f.write(lean_src + "\n")
        print(f"Wrote Lean Issuer snippet to {args.emit_lean}")

    if args.emit_py:
        py_src = emit_python_dataclass(issuer_fields)
        with open(args.emit_py, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
            f.write(py_src + "\n")
        print(f"Wrote Python IssuerModel snippet to {args.emit_py}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

```

Updated `infer_issuer_fields.py` to make field extraction and typing tighter.

- Field splitting now treats separators as tokens and trims comparators per part, so identifiers containing “and” aren’t shredded and siblings aren’t lost when one has a comparator.

```86:115:scripts/infer_issuer_fields.py
    re.IGNORECASE,
)
...

        for p in parts:

            # do not drop siblings joined by "and", "," etc.
            candidate = re.sub(r"\s*(>=|<=|=|>|<).*?$", "", p).strip()
```

- Type inference now reduces false Nats: narrower Bool prefixes with text-based cues, multi-year evidence defaults to `List Nat`, duration terms rely on field names (not global “years”), and new string heuristics capture names/types/identifiers.

**Cursor**

`ollama` missing just means the local Ollama server isn’t installed or on PATH. Options:

1) Install and start Ollama (local server at 11434):
- Download/install Ollama for Windows: https://ollama.com/download/windows
- Open a new terminal and run: `ollama serve`
- Verify: `ollama --version` and `curl http://localhost:11434/api/tags`
- Then re-run the `llm_generate_lean.py` command (it calls the HTTP API).

2) If you already installed but PATH isn’t updated, open a new terminal session after install, or run the full path to `ollama.exe` and start `ollama serve`.

3) If you prefer a remote/hosted LLM endpoint, we’d need to modify `llm_generate_lean.py` to hit that API instead of localhost:11434. Let me know and I’ll wire it up.

**User**

Can you go through and do the following after giving some thought?
## 0) Goal

From a single `rules_*.jsonl`:

1. generate a **stable schema** (IssuerFacts + OfferFacts)
2. generate a **complete RuleEvidence schema** (one entry per rule)
3. generate an **extraction plan** (how to extract each field/evidence)
4. produce a **scoring report** that explains gaps + what changed

---

## 1) Add deterministic “Rule Mapping Judge” report (no LLM)

### Create file: `scripts/score_rule_to_schema.py`

Inputs:

* `--rules_jsonl`
* `--issuer_fields_json` (optional, for regression comparison)

Outputs:

* `mapping_report.json`

Compute and output:

* `total_rules`
* `map_to_rules_count`
* `map_parse_success_count`
* `map_parse_failures`: list of `{rule_id, notes, dropped_tokens, reason}`
* `fields_proposed_from_map_to`: list
* `stability`: if run multiple times, Jaccard similarity (optional)
* `unmapped_rules`: list of rule_ids (no Map to)

This gives you a crisp diagnosis each run.

---

## 2) Make `infer_issuer_fields.py` deterministic and more robust

### Update in `infer_issuer_fields.py`

**(A) Normalize “Map to …” tokens**
Implement a helper:

* strip parentheses `(...)`
* strip trailing constraint fragments: `length=3`, comparisons, punctuation
* trim whitespace
* only then apply identifier regex

**(B) Do NOT depend on rule ordering**

* sort rules by `rule_id`
* sort final field list alphabetically before writing output

**(C) Emit debug metadata**
For every extracted field:

* `from_rule_ids`
* `raw_tokens_seen`
* `normalization_applied`

Output remains: `issuer_fields_*.json`, but now stable.

---

## 3) Generate “RuleEvidence” entries for *all rules* (guarantee coverage)

### Create file: `scripts/generate_rule_evidence_schema.py`

Inputs:

* `--rules_jsonl`

Output:

* `rule_evidence_schema.json`

For each rule_id, create an evidence template like:

```json
{
  "rule_id": "ICDR_17_c",
  "status_type": "enum(present, absent, unclear)",
  "evidence": [{"page": null, "quote": null}],
  "extracted_values": {},
  "notes": null
}
```

This ensures 40 rules ⇒ 40 evidence slots.

---

## 4) Introduce 2-layer facts schema: IssuerFacts + OfferFacts

### Create file: `scripts/build_facts_schema.py`

Inputs:

* `--rules_jsonl`
* `--issuer_fields_json` (from step 2)

Outputs:

* `facts_schema.json` with:

  * `issuer_facts`: fields + types
  * `offer_facts`: fields + types

**Heuristic routing:**

* If field name contains `offer`, `issue`, `fresh_issue`, `ofs`, `price_band`, `lot`, `listing`, `allocation`, `qib`, etc. ⇒ OfferFacts
* else ⇒ IssuerFacts

Keep it simple for now; you can refine later with LLM judges.

---

## 5) Add “promotion scoring” so only good facts become core schema

### Create file: `scripts/promote_fields.py`

Inputs:

* `--rules_jsonl`
* `--facts_schema_json`
* `--rule_evidence_schema_json`

Output:

* `promotion_report.json`
* updated `facts_schema.json`

Scoring (deterministic first):

* +2 if field appears in ≥2 rules (reusable)
* +2 if field has numeric threshold pattern nearby (extractable)
* -3 if token is generic (`conditions`, `misc`, `as_applicable`, `other`)
* -2 if only appears inside parentheses/notes with no clear anchor

If score < threshold: **demote** to evidence-only (keep rule coverage via RuleEvidence).

This keeps schema tight even if some rules are messy.

---

## 6) (Optional, later) Add LLM council judges *only for the hard cases*

### Create file: `scripts/judge_unmapped_or_lowconf.py`

Input: list of “unmapped rules” + “low score fields”
Output: `judge_suggestions.json`

Judges should propose:

* (a) evidence-only extraction (recommended evidence quotes)
* OR (b) a reusable fact field + type + extraction hint

But do this only after steps 1–5 stabilize outputs.

---

## 7) Generate Lean artifacts from the final schemas

### Update your Lean generation glue so it uses:

* `facts_schema.json` → generate `IssuerFacts` + `OfferFacts` structures (Lean)
* `rule_evidence_schema.json` → generate `RuleEvidence` structure (Lean)
* rules Lean file references these fields consistently

Add a compile gate:

* fail fast if schema changed but Lean generation didn’t compile

---

## 8) Add regression tests (quick, critical)

### Create `tests/test_schema_stability.py`

* Run inference twice on the same `rules_jsonl`
* Assert issuer field set is identical
* Assert evidence keys cover all rule_ids
* Assert all fields have valid Lean identifiers

---

## Deliverables Cursor should produce

**New files**

* `scripts/score_rule_to_schema.py`
* `scripts/generate_rule_evidence_schema.py`
* `scripts/build_facts_schema.py`
* `scripts/promote_fields.py`
* `tests/test_schema_stability.py`

**Modified**

* `infer_issuer_fields.py` (robust normalization + determinism + debug)

**Outputs from one run**

* `mapping_report.json`
* `issuer_fields.json` (stable)
* `facts_schema.json`
* `rule_evidence_schema.json`
* `promotion_report.json`

---

If you want the Cursor agent to be very effective, paste this instruction verbatim to it:

> “Implement steps 1–5 + test in step 8. Keep heuristics deterministic, no LLM calls yet. Ensure outputs are stable across runs and cover all rule_ids with RuleEvidence.”

That will generate the required code changes/files in a clean, incremental way.


---

**Cursor**

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
infer_issuer_fields.py

Scan rules JSONL files, extract candidate issuer field names from `notes`
(e.g. 'Map to holding_period >= 1 year.'), and infer a simple type
for each field from a small vocabulary:

  Bool, Nat, List Nat, String

Outputs a canonical issuer fields file that can be used to generate:

  - Lean `structure Issuer`
  - Python dataclass / Pydantic model
  - Extraction targets for the RHP parser

Usage (example):

  python scripts/infer_issuer_fields.py \
      --rules data/processed/rules_os_qwen_32b_v4_post_new.jsonl \
      --out data/processed/issuer_fields.json

"""

import argparse
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from typing import Dict, List, Iterable, Tuple, Optional


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Data structures
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

@dataclass
class FieldContext:
    """Aggregated info for one candidate issuer field."""
    name: str
    rule_ids: List[str]
    texts: List[str]      # small snippets from rules["text"]
    notes: List[str]      # full notes where it appeared
    raw_tokens: List[str]  # raw tokens seen prior to normalization
    normalizations: List[str]  # normalization steps applied

@dataclass
class IssuerField:
    """Final schema entry for an issuer field."""
    name: str
    lean_type: str
    python_type: str
    description: str
    from_rules: List[str]
    raw_tokens_seen: List[str]
    normalization_applied: List[str]


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Helpers to read rules JSONL
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def read_jsonl(path: str) -> Iterable[dict]:
    with open(path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        for line in f:
            line = line.strip()
            if not line:
                continue
            yield json.loads(line)


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Extract candidate field names from 'notes'
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

MAP_TO_RE = re.compile(
    r"""
    Map\sto          # literal 'Map to'
    \s+
    (?P<fields>.+?)  # everything until punctuation / end
    (?=$|[.;])
    """,
    re.IGNORECASE | re.VERBOSE
)

# Patterns inside the "fields" capture, e.g.
#  "holding_period"
#  "holding_period and capital_expenditure_utilization"
#  "promoter_lock_in, capital_expenditure_utilization"
# Use token-based separators so identifiers containing letters like
# "and" are not split apart.
SPLIT_FIELDS_RE = re.compile(
    r"\s*(?:,|\band/or\b|\band\b|\bor\b|/)\s*",
    re.IGNORECASE,
)


def extract_field_names_from_notes(notes: str) -> List[str]:
    """
    From a 'notes' string like:
      "Map to holding_period >= 1 year."
      "Map to promoter_lock_in and capital_expenditure_utilization."
    return ["holding_period", "promoter_lock_in", "capital_expenditure_utilization"]
    """
    result: List[str] = []
    for m in MAP_TO_RE.finditer(notes):
        fields_str = m.group("fields")
        parts = SPLIT_FIELDS_RE.split(fields_str)
        for p in parts:
            # Strip trailing comparators on each part individually so we
            # do not drop siblings joined by "and", "," etc.
            candidate = re.sub(r"\s*(>=|<=|=|>|<).*?$", "", p).strip()
            if not candidate:
                continue
            # keep only identifiers-like tokens
            if not re.match(r"^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*$", candidate):
                continue
            result.append(candidate)

    return result


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Aggregate field contexts
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def collect_field_contexts(rule_files: List[str]) -> Dict[str, FieldContext]:
    """
    Go through all rules in the given files, collect candidate field names
    from 'notes', and aggregate context.
    """
    fields: Dict[str, FieldContext] = {}

    for path in rule_files:
        for obj in read_jsonl(path):
            rule_id = obj.get("rule_id") or obj.get("rule_id_raw") or "UNKNOWN"
            notes = obj.get("notes") or ""
            text = obj.get("text") or ""

            field_names = extract_field_names_from_notes(notes)
            for fname in field_names:
                if fname not in fields:
                    fields[fname] = FieldContext(
                        name=fname,
                        rule_ids=[],
                        texts=[],
                        notes=[]
                    )
                ctx = fields[fname]
                if rule_id not in ctx.rule_ids:
                    ctx.rule_ids.append(rule_id)
                if text and text not in ctx.texts:
                    # keep only a few samples to avoid huge blobs
                    if len(ctx.texts) < 3:
                        ctx.texts.append(text)
                if notes and notes not in ctx.notes:
                    if len(ctx.notes) < 5:
                        ctx.notes.append(notes)

    return fields


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Heuristic type inference
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def guess_python_type(lean_type: str) -> str:
    if lean_type == "Bool":
        return "bool"
    if lean_type == "Nat":
        return "int"
    if lean_type == "List Nat":
        return "List[int]"
    if lean_type == "String":
        return "str"
    # fallback
    return "Any"


def infer_type_for_field(ctx: FieldContext) -> Tuple[str, str, str]:
    """
    Return (lean_type, python_type, description) for a field based on its name
    and a little bit of context.
    """
    name = ctx.name
    lname = name.lower()
    texts_concat = " ".join(ctx.texts).lower()
    notes_concat = " ".join(ctx.notes).lower()
    combined_text = f"{texts_concat} {notes_concat}"

    # --- Heuristic 1: Booleans by naming convention ---
    bool_prefixes = ("is_", "has_", "uses_")
    bool_text_cues = (
        "yes/no",
        "yes or no",
        "whether ",
        "shall not",
        "must not",
        "prohibited",
        "not permitted",
    )
    if lname.startswith(bool_prefixes) or any(cue in combined_text for cue in bool_text_cues):
        lean_type = "Bool"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Boolean flag: {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Heuristic 2: Ratios / percentages / counts -> Nat ---
    if ("ratio" in lname
        or "percent" in lname
        or lname.endswith("_pct")
        or lname.endswith("_count")
        or "per cent" in texts_concat
        or "%" in texts_concat):
        lean_type = "Nat"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Non-negative integer (e.g. percentage or ratio) for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Heuristic 3: multi-year numeric series -> List Nat ---
    has_years = ("preceding three years" in combined_text
                 or "preceding three full years" in combined_text
                 or "last three years" in combined_text
                 or "each of the preceding three" in combined_text)
    if has_years:
        lean_type = "List Nat"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Series of non-negative integers over multiple years for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Heuristic 4: contains 'period', 'months', 'years' -> Nat ---
    if (
        "period" in lname
        or "tenure" in lname
        or "duration" in lname
        or "months" in lname
        or "years" in lname
        or "age" in lname
        or "term" in lname
    ):
        lean_type = "Nat"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Non-negative integer duration (months/years) for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Heuristic 5: obvious strings / identifiers -> String ---
    string_name_keywords = (
        "name",
        "type",
        "category",
        "class",
        "sector",
        "industry",
        "country",
        "currency",
        "exchange",
        "isin",
        "cin",
        "pan",
        "tin",
        "address",
        "code",
        "rating",
        "identifier",
    )
    string_text_cues = (
        "name of",
        "shall be disclosed",
        "identify",
        "address",
    )
    if any(k in lname for k in string_name_keywords) or any(cue in combined_text for cue in string_text_cues):
        lean_type = "String"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Text/identifier field for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Fallback: treat as Nat (numeric) ---
    # You can later manually adjust a few to String/Enum if needed.
    lean_type = "Nat"
    py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
    descr = f"Non-negative integer field for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
    return lean_type, py_type, descr


def build_issuer_fields(
    field_contexts: Dict[str, FieldContext]
) -> List[IssuerField]:
    fields: List[IssuerField] = []
    for name, ctx in sorted(field_contexts.items()):
        lean_type, py_type, descr = infer_type_for_field(ctx)
        fields.append(
            IssuerField(
                name=name,
                lean_type=lean_type,
                python_type=py_type,
                description=descr,
                from_rules=sorted(ctx.rule_ids),
            )
        )
    return fields


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Pretty-print helpers (optional)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def emit_lean_structure(fields: List[IssuerField]) -> str:
    """
    Emit a Lean `structure Issuer` snippet as a convenience.
    You don't have to use this automatically; you can paste & edit.
    """
    lines = ["structure Issuer where"]
    for f in fields:
        lines.append(f"  {f.name} : {f.lean_type}")
    return "\n".join(lines)


def emit_python_dataclass(fields: List[IssuerField]) -> str:
    """
    Emit a Python @dataclass snippet as a convenience.
    """
    lines = [
        "from dataclasses import dataclass",
        "from typing import List, Optional",
        "",
        "@dataclass",
        "class IssuerModel:",
    ]
    for f in fields:
        # Map List[int] to proper typing
        py_type = f.python_type
        if py_type.startswith("List["):
            lines.append(f"  {f.name}: {py_type}  # {f.description}")
        else:
            lines.append(f"  {f.name}: {py_type}  # {f.description}")
    return "\n".join(lines)


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Main
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def main() -> None:
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    ap.add_argument(
        "--rules",
        nargs="+",
        required=True,
        help="One or more rules_*.jsonl files to scan for 'Map to ...' notes.",
    )
    ap.add_argument(
        "--out",
        required=True,
        help="Output path for issuer_fields.json (array of objects).",
    )
    ap.add_argument(
        "--emit-lean",
        default=None,
        help="Optional path to write a Lean `structure Issuer` snippet.",
    )
    ap.add_argument(
        "--emit-py",
        default=None,
        help="Optional path to write a Python dataclass snippet.",
    )
    args = ap.parse_args()

    # 1. collect field contexts
    ctxs = collect_field_contexts(args.rules)
    if not ctxs:
        print("No fields found via 'Map to ...' in the provided rules files.")
        return

    # 2. infer types
    issuer_fields = build_issuer_fields(ctxs)

    # 3. write issuer_fields.json
    out_data = [asdict(f) for f in issuer_fields]
    with open(args.out, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        json.dump(out_data, f, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2)
    print(f"Wrote {len(issuer_fields)} issuer fields to {args.out}")

    # 4. optionally emit helper snippets
    if args.emit_lean:
        lean_src = emit_lean_structure(issuer_fields)
        with open(args.emit_lean, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
            f.write(lean_src + "\n")
        print(f"Wrote Lean Issuer snippet to {args.emit_lean}")

    if args.emit_py:
        py_src = emit_python_dataclass(issuer_fields)
        with open(args.emit_py, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
            f.write(py_src + "\n")
        print(f"Wrote Python IssuerModel snippet to {args.emit_py}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

```

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
infer_issuer_fields.py

Scan rules JSONL files, extract candidate issuer field names from `notes`
(e.g. 'Map to holding_period >= 1 year.'), and infer a simple type
for each field from a small vocabulary:

  Bool, Nat, List Nat, String

Outputs a canonical issuer fields file that can be used to generate:

  - Lean `structure Issuer`
  - Python dataclass / Pydantic model
  - Extraction targets for the RHP parser

Usage (example):

  python scripts/infer_issuer_fields.py \
      --rules data/processed/rules_os_qwen_32b_v4_post_new.jsonl \
      --out data/processed/issuer_fields.json

"""

import argparse
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from typing import Dict, List, Iterable, Tuple, Optional


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Data structures
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

@dataclass
class FieldContext:
    """Aggregated info for one candidate issuer field."""
    name: str
    rule_ids: List[str]
    texts: List[str]      # small snippets from rules["text"]
    notes: List[str]      # full notes where it appeared
    raw_tokens: List[str]  # raw tokens seen prior to normalization
    normalizations: List[str]  # normalization steps applied

@dataclass
class IssuerField:
    """Final schema entry for an issuer field."""
    name: str
    lean_type: str
    python_type: str
    description: str
    from_rules: List[str]
    raw_tokens_seen: List[str]
    normalization_applied: List[str]


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Helpers to read rules JSONL
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def read_jsonl(path: str) -> Iterable[dict]:
    with open(path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        for line in f:
            line = line.strip()
            if not line:
                continue
            yield json.loads(line)


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Extract candidate field names from 'notes'
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

MAP_TO_RE = re.compile(
    r"""
    Map\sto          # literal 'Map to'
    \s+
    (?P<fields>.+?)  # everything until punctuation / end
    (?=$|[.;])
    """,
    re.IGNORECASE | re.VERBOSE
)

# Patterns inside the "fields" capture, e.g.
#  "holding_period"
#  "holding_period and capital_expenditure_utilization"
#  "promoter_lock_in, capital_expenditure_utilization"
# Use token-based separators so identifiers containing letters like
# "and" are not split apart.
SPLIT_FIELDS_RE = re.compile(
    r"\s*(?:,|\band/or\b|\band\b|\bor\b|/)\s*",
    re.IGNORECASE,
)


def normalize_field_token(token: str) -> Tuple[str, List[str]]:
    """
    Normalize a raw token extracted from 'Map to ...' by:
      - stripping outer parentheses
      - removing leftover paren characters
      - trimming trailing constraint fragments (comparators, length=3, etc.)
      - trimming punctuation
      - collapsing whitespace/hyphens to underscores
    Returns (normalized_token, [normalization_steps_applied]).
    """
    applied: List[str] = []
    t = token.strip()
    if not t:
        return "", applied

    # Strip balanced outer parentheses repeatedly
    changed = True
    while changed and t.startswith("(") and t.endswith(")") and len(t) > 1:
        changed = True
        t = t[1:-1].strip()
        applied.append("strip_outer_parens")
        # Check again if new string still wrapped in parens
        changed = t.startswith("(") and t.endswith(")")

    # Remove any remaining paren characters
    if "(" in t or ")" in t:
        t = re.sub(r"[()]", " ", t)
        applied.append("drop_paren_chars")

    # Drop trailing comparator / constraint fragments
    new_t = re.sub(r"\s*(?:length\s*=\s*\d+|>=|<=|=|>|<).*$", "", t)
    if new_t != t:
        t = new_t
        applied.append("drop_comparator_or_length")

    # Trim trailing punctuation
    new_t = t.strip(" ,.;:")
    if new_t != t:
        t = new_t
        applied.append("trim_punct")

    # Collapse whitespace or hyphens to underscore
    new_t = re.sub(r"[\s\-]+", "_", t)
    if new_t != t:
        t = new_t
        applied.append("collapse_space_hyphen")

    t = t.strip("_")
    if not t:
        return "", applied

    return t, applied


def extract_field_names_from_notes(notes: str) -> List[str]:
    """
    From a 'notes' string like:
      "Map to holding_period >= 1 year."
      "Map to promoter_lock_in and capital_expenditure_utilization."
    return ["holding_period", "promoter_lock_in", "capital_expenditure_utilization"]
    """
    result: List[str] = []
    for m in MAP_TO_RE.finditer(notes):
        fields_str = m.group("fields")
        parts = SPLIT_FIELDS_RE.split(fields_str)
        for p in parts:
            # Strip trailing comparators on each part individually so we
            # do not drop siblings joined by "and", "," etc.
            candidate = re.sub(r"\s*(>=|<=|=|>|<).*?$", "", p).strip()
            if not candidate:
                continue
            # keep only identifiers-like tokens
            if not re.match(r"^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*$", candidate):
                continue
            result.append(candidate)

    return result


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Aggregate field contexts
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def collect_field_contexts(rule_files: List[str]) -> Dict[str, FieldContext]:
    """
    Go through all rules in the given files, collect candidate field names
    from 'notes', and aggregate context.
    """
    fields: Dict[str, FieldContext] = {}

    for path in rule_files:
        for obj in read_jsonl(path):
            rule_id = obj.get("rule_id") or obj.get("rule_id_raw") or "UNKNOWN"
            notes = obj.get("notes") or ""
            text = obj.get("text") or ""

            field_names = extract_field_names_from_notes(notes)
            for fname in field_names:
                if fname not in fields:
                    fields[fname] = FieldContext(
                        name=fname,
                        rule_ids=[],
                        texts=[],
                        notes=[]
                    )
                ctx = fields[fname]
                if rule_id not in ctx.rule_ids:
                    ctx.rule_ids.append(rule_id)
                if text and text not in ctx.texts:
                    # keep only a few samples to avoid huge blobs
                    if len(ctx.texts) < 3:
                        ctx.texts.append(text)
                if notes and notes not in ctx.notes:
                    if len(ctx.notes) < 5:
                        ctx.notes.append(notes)

    return fields


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Heuristic type inference
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def guess_python_type(lean_type: str) -> str:
    if lean_type == "Bool":
        return "bool"
    if lean_type == "Nat":
        return "int"
    if lean_type == "List Nat":
        return "List[int]"
    if lean_type == "String":
        return "str"
    # fallback
    return "Any"


def infer_type_for_field(ctx: FieldContext) -> Tuple[str, str, str]:
    """
    Return (lean_type, python_type, description) for a field based on its name
    and a little bit of context.
    """
    name = ctx.name
    lname = name.lower()
    texts_concat = " ".join(ctx.texts).lower()
    notes_concat = " ".join(ctx.notes).lower()
    combined_text = f"{texts_concat} {notes_concat}"

    # --- Heuristic 1: Booleans by naming convention ---
    bool_prefixes = ("is_", "has_", "uses_")
    bool_text_cues = (
        "yes/no",
        "yes or no",
        "whether ",
        "shall not",
        "must not",
        "prohibited",
        "not permitted",
    )
    if lname.startswith(bool_prefixes) or any(cue in combined_text for cue in bool_text_cues):
        lean_type = "Bool"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Boolean flag: {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Heuristic 2: Ratios / percentages / counts -> Nat ---
    if ("ratio" in lname
        or "percent" in lname
        or lname.endswith("_pct")
        or lname.endswith("_count")
        or "per cent" in texts_concat
        or "%" in texts_concat):
        lean_type = "Nat"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Non-negative integer (e.g. percentage or ratio) for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Heuristic 3: multi-year numeric series -> List Nat ---
    has_years = ("preceding three years" in combined_text
                 or "preceding three full years" in combined_text
                 or "last three years" in combined_text
                 or "each of the preceding three" in combined_text)
    if has_years:
        lean_type = "List Nat"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Series of non-negative integers over multiple years for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Heuristic 4: contains 'period', 'months', 'years' -> Nat ---
    if (
        "period" in lname
        or "tenure" in lname
        or "duration" in lname
        or "months" in lname
        or "years" in lname
        or "age" in lname
        or "term" in lname
    ):
        lean_type = "Nat"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Non-negative integer duration (months/years) for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Heuristic 5: obvious strings / identifiers -> String ---
    string_name_keywords = (
        "name",
        "type",
        "category",
        "class",
        "sector",
        "industry",
        "country",
        "currency",
        "exchange",
        "isin",
        "cin",
        "pan",
        "tin",
        "address",
        "code",
        "rating",
        "identifier",
    )
    string_text_cues = (
        "name of",
        "shall be disclosed",
        "identify",
        "address",
    )
    if any(k in lname for k in string_name_keywords) or any(cue in combined_text for cue in string_text_cues):
        lean_type = "String"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Text/identifier field for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Fallback: treat as Nat (numeric) ---
    # You can later manually adjust a few to String/Enum if needed.
    lean_type = "Nat"
    py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
    descr = f"Non-negative integer field for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
    return lean_type, py_type, descr


def build_issuer_fields(
    field_contexts: Dict[str, FieldContext]
) -> List[IssuerField]:
    fields: List[IssuerField] = []
    for name, ctx in sorted(field_contexts.items()):
        lean_type, py_type, descr = infer_type_for_field(ctx)
        fields.append(
            IssuerField(
                name=name,
                lean_type=lean_type,
                python_type=py_type,
                description=descr,
                from_rules=sorted(ctx.rule_ids),
            )
        )
    return fields


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Pretty-print helpers (optional)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def emit_lean_structure(fields: List[IssuerField]) -> str:
    """
    Emit a Lean `structure Issuer` snippet as a convenience.
    You don't have to use this automatically; you can paste & edit.
    """
    lines = ["structure Issuer where"]
    for f in fields:
        lines.append(f"  {f.name} : {f.lean_type}")
    return "\n".join(lines)


def emit_python_dataclass(fields: List[IssuerField]) -> str:
    """
    Emit a Python @dataclass snippet as a convenience.
    """
    lines = [
        "from dataclasses import dataclass",
        "from typing import List, Optional",
        "",
        "@dataclass",
        "class IssuerModel:",
    ]
    for f in fields:
        # Map List[int] to proper typing
        py_type = f.python_type
        if py_type.startswith("List["):
            lines.append(f"  {f.name}: {py_type}  # {f.description}")
        else:
            lines.append(f"  {f.name}: {py_type}  # {f.description}")
    return "\n".join(lines)


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Main
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def main() -> None:
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    ap.add_argument(
        "--rules",
        nargs="+",
        required=True,
        help="One or more rules_*.jsonl files to scan for 'Map to ...' notes.",
    )
    ap.add_argument(
        "--out",
        required=True,
        help="Output path for issuer_fields.json (array of objects).",
    )
    ap.add_argument(
        "--emit-lean",
        default=None,
        help="Optional path to write a Lean `structure Issuer` snippet.",
    )
    ap.add_argument(
        "--emit-py",
        default=None,
        help="Optional path to write a Python dataclass snippet.",
    )
    args = ap.parse_args()

    # 1. collect field contexts
    ctxs = collect_field_contexts(args.rules)
    if not ctxs:
        print("No fields found via 'Map to ...' in the provided rules files.")
        return

    # 2. infer types
    issuer_fields = build_issuer_fields(ctxs)

    # 3. write issuer_fields.json
    out_data = [asdict(f) for f in issuer_fields]
    with open(args.out, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        json.dump(out_data, f, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2)
    print(f"Wrote {len(issuer_fields)} issuer fields to {args.out}")

    # 4. optionally emit helper snippets
    if args.emit_lean:
        lean_src = emit_lean_structure(issuer_fields)
        with open(args.emit_lean, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
            f.write(lean_src + "\n")
        print(f"Wrote Lean Issuer snippet to {args.emit_lean}")

    if args.emit_py:
        py_src = emit_python_dataclass(issuer_fields)
        with open(args.emit_py, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
            f.write(py_src + "\n")
        print(f"Wrote Python IssuerModel snippet to {args.emit_py}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

```

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
infer_issuer_fields.py

Scan rules JSONL files, extract candidate issuer field names from `notes`
(e.g. 'Map to holding_period >= 1 year.'), and infer a simple type
for each field from a small vocabulary:

  Bool, Nat, List Nat, String

Outputs a canonical issuer fields file that can be used to generate:

  - Lean `structure Issuer`
  - Python dataclass / Pydantic model
  - Extraction targets for the RHP parser

Usage (example):

  python scripts/infer_issuer_fields.py \
      --rules data/processed/rules_os_qwen_32b_v4_post_new.jsonl \
      --out data/processed/issuer_fields.json

"""

import argparse
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from typing import Dict, List, Iterable, Tuple, Optional


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Data structures
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

@dataclass
class FieldContext:
    """Aggregated info for one candidate issuer field."""
    name: str
    rule_ids: List[str]
    texts: List[str]      # small snippets from rules["text"]
    notes: List[str]      # full notes where it appeared
    raw_tokens: List[str]  # raw tokens seen prior to normalization
    normalizations: List[str]  # normalization steps applied

@dataclass
class IssuerField:
    """Final schema entry for an issuer field."""
    name: str
    lean_type: str
    python_type: str
    description: str
    from_rules: List[str]
    raw_tokens_seen: List[str]
    normalization_applied: List[str]


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Helpers to read rules JSONL
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def read_jsonl(path: str) -> Iterable[dict]:
    with open(path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        for line in f:
            line = line.strip()
            if not line:
                continue
            yield json.loads(line)


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Extract candidate field names from 'notes'
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

MAP_TO_RE = re.compile(
    r"""
    Map\sto          # literal 'Map to'
    \s+
    (?P<fields>.+?)  # everything until punctuation / end
    (?=$|[.;])
    """,
    re.IGNORECASE | re.VERBOSE
)

# Patterns inside the "fields" capture, e.g.
#  "holding_period"
#  "holding_period and capital_expenditure_utilization"
#  "promoter_lock_in, capital_expenditure_utilization"
# Use token-based separators so identifiers containing letters like
# "and" are not split apart.
SPLIT_FIELDS_RE = re.compile(
    r"\s*(?:,|\band/or\b|\band\b|\bor\b|/)\s*",
    re.IGNORECASE,
)


def normalize_field_token(token: str) -> Tuple[str, List[str]]:
    """
    Normalize a raw token extracted from 'Map to ...' by:
      - stripping outer parentheses
      - removing leftover paren characters
      - trimming trailing constraint fragments (comparators, length=3, etc.)
      - trimming punctuation
      - collapsing whitespace/hyphens to underscores
    Returns (normalized_token, [normalization_steps_applied]).
    """
    applied: List[str] = []
    t = token.strip()
    if not t:
        return "", applied

    # Strip balanced outer parentheses repeatedly
    changed = True
    while changed and t.startswith("(") and t.endswith(")") and len(t) > 1:
        changed = True
        t = t[1:-1].strip()
        applied.append("strip_outer_parens")
        # Check again if new string still wrapped in parens
        changed = t.startswith("(") and t.endswith(")")

    # Remove any remaining paren characters
    if "(" in t or ")" in t:
        t = re.sub(r"[()]", " ", t)
        applied.append("drop_paren_chars")

    # Drop trailing comparator / constraint fragments
    new_t = re.sub(r"\s*(?:length\s*=\s*\d+|>=|<=|=|>|<).*$", "", t)
    if new_t != t:
        t = new_t
        applied.append("drop_comparator_or_length")

    # Trim trailing punctuation
    new_t = t.strip(" ,.;:")
    if new_t != t:
        t = new_t
        applied.append("trim_punct")

    # Collapse whitespace or hyphens to underscore
    new_t = re.sub(r"[\s\-]+", "_", t)
    if new_t != t:
        t = new_t
        applied.append("collapse_space_hyphen")

    t = t.strip("_")
    if not t:
        return "", applied

    return t, applied


def extract_field_names_from_notes(notes: str) -> List[Tuple[str, str, List[str]]]:
    """
    From a 'notes' string like:
      "Map to holding_period >= 1 year."
      "Map to promoter_lock_in and capital_expenditure_utilization."
    return list of tuples (normalized_field, raw_token, normalization_steps)
    """
    result: List[Tuple[str, str, List[str]]] = []
    for m in MAP_TO_RE.finditer(notes):
        fields_str = m.group("fields")
        parts = SPLIT_FIELDS_RE.split(fields_str)
        for p in parts:
            # Strip trailing comparators on each part individually so we
            # do not drop siblings joined by "and", "," etc.
            candidate_raw = re.sub(r"\s*(>=|<=|=|>|<).*?$", "", p).strip()
            if not candidate_raw:
                continue
            normalized, applied = normalize_field_token(candidate_raw)
            # keep only identifiers-like tokens
            if not normalized or not re.match(r"^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*$", normalized):
                continue
            result.append((normalized, candidate_raw, applied))

    return result


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Aggregate field contexts
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def collect_field_contexts(rule_files: List[str]) -> Dict[str, FieldContext]:
    """
    Go through all rules in the given files, collect candidate field names
    from 'notes', and aggregate context.
    """
    fields: Dict[str, FieldContext] = {}

    for path in rule_files:
        for obj in read_jsonl(path):
            rule_id = obj.get("rule_id") or obj.get("rule_id_raw") or "UNKNOWN"
            notes = obj.get("notes") or ""
            text = obj.get("text") or ""

            field_names = extract_field_names_from_notes(notes)
            for fname in field_names:
                if fname not in fields:
                    fields[fname] = FieldContext(
                        name=fname,
                        rule_ids=[],
                        texts=[],
                        notes=[]
                    )
                ctx = fields[fname]
                if rule_id not in ctx.rule_ids:
                    ctx.rule_ids.append(rule_id)
                if text and text not in ctx.texts:
                    # keep only a few samples to avoid huge blobs
                    if len(ctx.texts) < 3:
                        ctx.texts.append(text)
                if notes and notes not in ctx.notes:
                    if len(ctx.notes) < 5:
                        ctx.notes.append(notes)

    return fields


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Heuristic type inference
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def guess_python_type(lean_type: str) -> str:
    if lean_type == "Bool":
        return "bool"
    if lean_type == "Nat":
        return "int"
    if lean_type == "List Nat":
        return "List[int]"
    if lean_type == "String":
        return "str"
    # fallback
    return "Any"


def infer_type_for_field(ctx: FieldContext) -> Tuple[str, str, str]:
    """
    Return (lean_type, python_type, description) for a field based on its name
    and a little bit of context.
    """
    name = ctx.name
    lname = name.lower()
    texts_concat = " ".join(ctx.texts).lower()
    notes_concat = " ".join(ctx.notes).lower()
    combined_text = f"{texts_concat} {notes_concat}"

    # --- Heuristic 1: Booleans by naming convention ---
    bool_prefixes = ("is_", "has_", "uses_")
    bool_text_cues = (
        "yes/no",
        "yes or no",
        "whether ",
        "shall not",
        "must not",
        "prohibited",
        "not permitted",
    )
    if lname.startswith(bool_prefixes) or any(cue in combined_text for cue in bool_text_cues):
        lean_type = "Bool"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Boolean flag: {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Heuristic 2: Ratios / percentages / counts -> Nat ---
    if ("ratio" in lname
        or "percent" in lname
        or lname.endswith("_pct")
        or lname.endswith("_count")
        or "per cent" in texts_concat
        or "%" in texts_concat):
        lean_type = "Nat"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Non-negative integer (e.g. percentage or ratio) for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Heuristic 3: multi-year numeric series -> List Nat ---
    has_years = ("preceding three years" in combined_text
                 or "preceding three full years" in combined_text
                 or "last three years" in combined_text
                 or "each of the preceding three" in combined_text)
    if has_years:
        lean_type = "List Nat"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Series of non-negative integers over multiple years for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Heuristic 4: contains 'period', 'months', 'years' -> Nat ---
    if (
        "period" in lname
        or "tenure" in lname
        or "duration" in lname
        or "months" in lname
        or "years" in lname
        or "age" in lname
        or "term" in lname
    ):
        lean_type = "Nat"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Non-negative integer duration (months/years) for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Heuristic 5: obvious strings / identifiers -> String ---
    string_name_keywords = (
        "name",
        "type",
        "category",
        "class",
        "sector",
        "industry",
        "country",
        "currency",
        "exchange",
        "isin",
        "cin",
        "pan",
        "tin",
        "address",
        "code",
        "rating",
        "identifier",
    )
    string_text_cues = (
        "name of",
        "shall be disclosed",
        "identify",
        "address",
    )
    if any(k in lname for k in string_name_keywords) or any(cue in combined_text for cue in string_text_cues):
        lean_type = "String"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Text/identifier field for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Fallback: treat as Nat (numeric) ---
    # You can later manually adjust a few to String/Enum if needed.
    lean_type = "Nat"
    py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
    descr = f"Non-negative integer field for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
    return lean_type, py_type, descr


def build_issuer_fields(
    field_contexts: Dict[str, FieldContext]
) -> List[IssuerField]:
    fields: List[IssuerField] = []
    for name, ctx in sorted(field_contexts.items()):
        lean_type, py_type, descr = infer_type_for_field(ctx)
        fields.append(
            IssuerField(
                name=name,
                lean_type=lean_type,
                python_type=py_type,
                description=descr,
                from_rules=sorted(ctx.rule_ids),
            )
        )
    return fields


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Pretty-print helpers (optional)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def emit_lean_structure(fields: List[IssuerField]) -> str:
    """
    Emit a Lean `structure Issuer` snippet as a convenience.
    You don't have to use this automatically; you can paste & edit.
    """
    lines = ["structure Issuer where"]
    for f in fields:
        lines.append(f"  {f.name} : {f.lean_type}")
    return "\n".join(lines)


def emit_python_dataclass(fields: List[IssuerField]) -> str:
    """
    Emit a Python @dataclass snippet as a convenience.
    """
    lines = [
        "from dataclasses import dataclass",
        "from typing import List, Optional",
        "",
        "@dataclass",
        "class IssuerModel:",
    ]
    for f in fields:
        # Map List[int] to proper typing
        py_type = f.python_type
        if py_type.startswith("List["):
            lines.append(f"  {f.name}: {py_type}  # {f.description}")
        else:
            lines.append(f"  {f.name}: {py_type}  # {f.description}")
    return "\n".join(lines)


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Main
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def main() -> None:
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    ap.add_argument(
        "--rules",
        nargs="+",
        required=True,
        help="One or more rules_*.jsonl files to scan for 'Map to ...' notes.",
    )
    ap.add_argument(
        "--out",
        required=True,
        help="Output path for issuer_fields.json (array of objects).",
    )
    ap.add_argument(
        "--emit-lean",
        default=None,
        help="Optional path to write a Lean `structure Issuer` snippet.",
    )
    ap.add_argument(
        "--emit-py",
        default=None,
        help="Optional path to write a Python dataclass snippet.",
    )
    args = ap.parse_args()

    # 1. collect field contexts
    ctxs = collect_field_contexts(args.rules)
    if not ctxs:
        print("No fields found via 'Map to ...' in the provided rules files.")
        return

    # 2. infer types
    issuer_fields = build_issuer_fields(ctxs)

    # 3. write issuer_fields.json
    out_data = [asdict(f) for f in issuer_fields]
    with open(args.out, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        json.dump(out_data, f, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2)
    print(f"Wrote {len(issuer_fields)} issuer fields to {args.out}")

    # 4. optionally emit helper snippets
    if args.emit_lean:
        lean_src = emit_lean_structure(issuer_fields)
        with open(args.emit_lean, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
            f.write(lean_src + "\n")
        print(f"Wrote Lean Issuer snippet to {args.emit_lean}")

    if args.emit_py:
        py_src = emit_python_dataclass(issuer_fields)
        with open(args.emit_py, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
            f.write(py_src + "\n")
        print(f"Wrote Python IssuerModel snippet to {args.emit_py}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

```

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
infer_issuer_fields.py

Scan rules JSONL files, extract candidate issuer field names from `notes`
(e.g. 'Map to holding_period >= 1 year.'), and infer a simple type
for each field from a small vocabulary:

  Bool, Nat, List Nat, String

Outputs a canonical issuer fields file that can be used to generate:

  - Lean `structure Issuer`
  - Python dataclass / Pydantic model
  - Extraction targets for the RHP parser

Usage (example):

  python scripts/infer_issuer_fields.py \
      --rules data/processed/rules_os_qwen_32b_v4_post_new.jsonl \
      --out data/processed/issuer_fields.json

"""

import argparse
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from typing import Dict, List, Iterable, Tuple, Optional


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Data structures
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

@dataclass
class FieldContext:
    """Aggregated info for one candidate issuer field."""
    name: str
    rule_ids: List[str]
    texts: List[str]      # small snippets from rules["text"]
    notes: List[str]      # full notes where it appeared
    raw_tokens: List[str]  # raw tokens seen prior to normalization
    normalizations: List[str]  # normalization steps applied

@dataclass
class IssuerField:
    """Final schema entry for an issuer field."""
    name: str
    lean_type: str
    python_type: str
    description: str
    from_rules: List[str]
    raw_tokens_seen: List[str]
    normalization_applied: List[str]


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Helpers to read rules JSONL
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def read_jsonl(path: str) -> Iterable[dict]:
    with open(path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        for line in f:
            line = line.strip()
            if not line:
                continue
            yield json.loads(line)


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Extract candidate field names from 'notes'
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

MAP_TO_RE = re.compile(
    r"""
    Map\sto          # literal 'Map to'
    \s+
    (?P<fields>.+?)  # everything until punctuation / end
    (?=$|[.;])
    """,
    re.IGNORECASE | re.VERBOSE
)

# Patterns inside the "fields" capture, e.g.
#  "holding_period"
#  "holding_period and capital_expenditure_utilization"
#  "promoter_lock_in, capital_expenditure_utilization"
# Use token-based separators so identifiers containing letters like
# "and" are not split apart.
SPLIT_FIELDS_RE = re.compile(
    r"\s*(?:,|\band/or\b|\band\b|\bor\b|/)\s*",
    re.IGNORECASE,
)


def normalize_field_token(token: str) -> Tuple[str, List[str]]:
    """
    Normalize a raw token extracted from 'Map to ...' by:
      - stripping outer parentheses
      - removing leftover paren characters
      - trimming trailing constraint fragments (comparators, length=3, etc.)
      - trimming punctuation
      - collapsing whitespace/hyphens to underscores
    Returns (normalized_token, [normalization_steps_applied]).
    """
    applied: List[str] = []
    t = token.strip()
    if not t:
        return "", applied

    # Strip balanced outer parentheses repeatedly
    changed = True
    while changed and t.startswith("(") and t.endswith(")") and len(t) > 1:
        changed = True
        t = t[1:-1].strip()
        applied.append("strip_outer_parens")
        # Check again if new string still wrapped in parens
        changed = t.startswith("(") and t.endswith(")")

    # Remove any remaining paren characters
    if "(" in t or ")" in t:
        t = re.sub(r"[()]", " ", t)
        applied.append("drop_paren_chars")

    # Drop trailing comparator / constraint fragments
    new_t = re.sub(r"\s*(?:length\s*=\s*\d+|>=|<=|=|>|<).*$", "", t)
    if new_t != t:
        t = new_t
        applied.append("drop_comparator_or_length")

    # Trim trailing punctuation
    new_t = t.strip(" ,.;:")
    if new_t != t:
        t = new_t
        applied.append("trim_punct")

    # Collapse whitespace or hyphens to underscore
    new_t = re.sub(r"[\s\-]+", "_", t)
    if new_t != t:
        t = new_t
        applied.append("collapse_space_hyphen")

    t = t.strip("_")
    if not t:
        return "", applied

    return t, applied


def extract_field_names_from_notes(notes: str) -> List[Tuple[str, str, List[str]]]:
    """
    From a 'notes' string like:
      "Map to holding_period >= 1 year."
      "Map to promoter_lock_in and capital_expenditure_utilization."
    return list of tuples (normalized_field, raw_token, normalization_steps)
    """
    result: List[Tuple[str, str, List[str]]] = []
    for m in MAP_TO_RE.finditer(notes):
        fields_str = m.group("fields")
        parts = SPLIT_FIELDS_RE.split(fields_str)
        for p in parts:
            # Strip trailing comparators on each part individually so we
            # do not drop siblings joined by "and", "," etc.
            candidate_raw = re.sub(r"\s*(>=|<=|=|>|<).*?$", "", p).strip()
            if not candidate_raw:
                continue
            normalized, applied = normalize_field_token(candidate_raw)
            # keep only identifiers-like tokens
            if not normalized or not re.match(r"^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*$", normalized):
                continue
            result.append((normalized, candidate_raw, applied))

    return result


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Aggregate field contexts
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def collect_field_contexts(rule_files: List[str]) -> Dict[str, FieldContext]:
    """
    Go through all rules in the given files, collect candidate field names
    from 'notes', and aggregate context.
    """
    fields: Dict[str, FieldContext] = {}

    for path in rule_files:
        objs = list(read_jsonl(path))
        # Sort deterministically by rule_id (string compare)
        def _rid(o: dict) -> str:
            rid = o.get("rule_id") or o.get("rule_id_raw")
            if rid is None:
                return ""
            return str(rid)

        objs.sort(key=_rid)

        for obj in objs:
            rule_id = obj.get("rule_id") or obj.get("rule_id_raw") or "UNKNOWN"
            notes = obj.get("notes") or ""
            text = obj.get("text") or ""

            field_infos = extract_field_names_from_notes(notes)
            for fname, raw_tok, applied_norms in field_infos:
                if fname not in fields:
                    fields[fname] = FieldContext(
                        name=fname,
                        rule_ids=[],
                        texts=[],
                        notes=[],
                        raw_tokens=[],
                        normalizations=[],
                    )
                ctx = fields[fname]
                if rule_id not in ctx.rule_ids:
                    ctx.rule_ids.append(rule_id)
                if text and text not in ctx.texts:
                    # keep only a few samples to avoid huge blobs
                    if len(ctx.texts) < 3:
                        ctx.texts.append(text)
                if notes and notes not in ctx.notes:
                    if len(ctx.notes) < 5:
                        ctx.notes.append(notes)
                if raw_tok not in ctx.raw_tokens:
                    ctx.raw_tokens.append(raw_tok)
                for step in applied_norms:
                    if step not in ctx.normalizations:
                        ctx.normalizations.append(step)

    return fields


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Heuristic type inference
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def guess_python_type(lean_type: str) -> str:
    if lean_type == "Bool":
        return "bool"
    if lean_type == "Nat":
        return "int"
    if lean_type == "List Nat":
        return "List[int]"
    if lean_type == "String":
        return "str"
    # fallback
    return "Any"


def infer_type_for_field(ctx: FieldContext) -> Tuple[str, str, str]:
    """
    Return (lean_type, python_type, description) for a field based on its name
    and a little bit of context.
    """
    name = ctx.name
    lname = name.lower()
    texts_concat = " ".join(ctx.texts).lower()
    notes_concat = " ".join(ctx.notes).lower()
    combined_text = f"{texts_concat} {notes_concat}"

    # --- Heuristic 1: Booleans by naming convention ---
    bool_prefixes = ("is_", "has_", "uses_")
    bool_text_cues = (
        "yes/no",
        "yes or no",
        "whether ",
        "shall not",
        "must not",
        "prohibited",
        "not permitted",
    )
    if lname.startswith(bool_prefixes) or any(cue in combined_text for cue in bool_text_cues):
        lean_type = "Bool"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Boolean flag: {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Heuristic 2: Ratios / percentages / counts -> Nat ---
    if ("ratio" in lname
        or "percent" in lname
        or lname.endswith("_pct")
        or lname.endswith("_count")
        or "per cent" in texts_concat
        or "%" in texts_concat):
        lean_type = "Nat"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Non-negative integer (e.g. percentage or ratio) for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Heuristic 3: multi-year numeric series -> List Nat ---
    has_years = ("preceding three years" in combined_text
                 or "preceding three full years" in combined_text
                 or "last three years" in combined_text
                 or "each of the preceding three" in combined_text)
    if has_years:
        lean_type = "List Nat"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Series of non-negative integers over multiple years for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Heuristic 4: contains 'period', 'months', 'years' -> Nat ---
    if (
        "period" in lname
        or "tenure" in lname
        or "duration" in lname
        or "months" in lname
        or "years" in lname
        or "age" in lname
        or "term" in lname
    ):
        lean_type = "Nat"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Non-negative integer duration (months/years) for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Heuristic 5: obvious strings / identifiers -> String ---
    string_name_keywords = (
        "name",
        "type",
        "category",
        "class",
        "sector",
        "industry",
        "country",
        "currency",
        "exchange",
        "isin",
        "cin",
        "pan",
        "tin",
        "address",
        "code",
        "rating",
        "identifier",
    )
    string_text_cues = (
        "name of",
        "shall be disclosed",
        "identify",
        "address",
    )
    if any(k in lname for k in string_name_keywords) or any(cue in combined_text for cue in string_text_cues):
        lean_type = "String"
        py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
        descr = f"Text/identifier field for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
        return lean_type, py_type, descr

    # --- Fallback: treat as Nat (numeric) ---
    # You can later manually adjust a few to String/Enum if needed.
    lean_type = "Nat"
    py_type = guess_python_type(lean_type)
    descr = f"Non-negative integer field for {name.replace('_', ' ')}."
    return lean_type, py_type, descr


def build_issuer_fields(
    field_contexts: Dict[str, FieldContext]
) -> List[IssuerField]:
    fields: List[IssuerField] = []
    for name, ctx in sorted(field_contexts.items()):
        lean_type, py_type, descr = infer_type_for_field(ctx)
        fields.append(
            IssuerField(
                name=name,
                lean_type=lean_type,
                python_type=py_type,
                description=descr,
                from_rules=sorted(ctx.rule_ids),
            )
        )
    return fields


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Pretty-print helpers (optional)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------

def emit_lean_structure(fields: List[IssuerField]) -> str:
    """
    Emit a Lean `structure Issuer` snippet as a convenience.
    You don't have to use this automatically; you can paste & edit.
    """
    lines = ["structure Issuer where"]
    for f in fields:
        lines.append(f"  {f.name} : {f.lean_type}")
    return "\n".join(lines)


def emit_python_dataclass(fields: List[IssuerField]) -> str:
    """
    Emit a Python @dataclass snippet as a convenience.
    """
    lines = [
```
